questions.question: What happens to lift as angle of attack increases below the critical angle of attack?
questions.explanation: Below the critical angle of attack, increasing AoA increases the pressure differential between the upper and lower wing surfaces, producing more lift.

questions.question: An airplane can stall at any airspeed and any attitude. What determines whether a stall occurs?
questions.correct_answer: Exceeding the critical angle of attack
distractors: Flying with the nose above the horizon
questions.explanation: A stall occurs when the critical angle of attack is exceeded, regardless of airspeed, attitude, or weight.

questions.question: Which type of drag increases as airspeed increases?
distractors: Profile drag decreases at all speeds
questions.explanation: Parasite drag increases with the square of airspeed, while induced drag decreases as airspeed increases.

questions.question: What is the primary effect of ground effect on an aircraft during takeoff?
questions.explanation: Ground effect reduces the upwash and downwash around the wing, decreasing induced drag. It is most pronounced within one wingspan of the surface.

questions.question: In a coordinated level turn, what must increase to maintain altitude?
questions.explanation: In a banked turn, the vertical component of lift decreases. Total lift must increase (by increasing AoA/back pressure) to maintain the same vertical lift component and hold altitude.

questions.question: What is the load factor in a 60-degree banked level turn?
questions.explanation: Load factor = 1/cos(bank angle). At 60 degrees: 1/cos(60°) = 1/0.5 = 2 Gs.

questions.question: What does positive static stability mean?
questions.correct_answer: The aircraft tends to return to its original state after a disturbance
distractors: The aircraft remains in its displaced position
The aircraft continues to diverge from its original state
The aircraft oscillates with increasing amplitude
questions.explanation: Positive static stability means the initial tendency is to return toward the original state of equilibrium after being disturbed.

questions.question: Which of the four forces acts parallel to the flight path and opposite to the direction of flight?
questions.explanation: Drag acts rearward, parallel to the relative wind and opposite to the direction of flight.

questions.question: Why do some wings have washout (twist)?
questions.correct_answer: To ensure the wing root stalls before the wingtip, maintaining aileron effectiveness
distractors: To increase the maximum lift coefficient
To reduce parasite drag at cruise speeds
To allow higher speeds before flutter occurs
questions.explanation: Washout decreases the angle of incidence from root to tip so the root reaches the critical AoA first, keeping ailerons effective during the stall.

questions.question: What is the relationship between indicated airspeed and true airspeed as altitude increases?
questions.correct_answer: True airspeed is higher than indicated airspeed
distractors: True airspeed is lower than indicated airspeed
The relationship depends solely on temperature
questions.explanation: As altitude increases, air density decreases. The pitot-static system senses less dynamic pressure, so IAS reads lower than the actual TAS.
